curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: pie charts (fractions, percentages and angles for categorical data)
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Interpret and construct pie charts; convert between frequency, fraction, percentage and angle; use proportional reasoning to describe and compare sectors, especially in SDG contexts.

verse_ality.driving_question: How can a circle show whether the world is being shared fairly?

knowing_world.focus: Use pie charts to represent categorical data as fractions, percentages and angles, and practise both constructing and interpreting SDG-themed pie charts.
key_concepts: Total of a pie chart (100% and 360°)
tools: Interactive: Pie Chart Builder (SDG Data & Angles) (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAG_aqCoBYk/uLNHjvytk_Qx_AbH_obcvg/view?embed
Interactive: Read the Circle (SDG Pie Chart Explorer) (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAG_gkwydIc/ouISkcnY5TW4bFrHIrVQMw/view?embed

human_story.focus: Pie charts are about shares: who or what gets which portion of limited resources like time, money, energy, land, and attention. In SDG contexts, pie charts can reveal priorities, trade-offs, and who might be harmed or helped by how the ‘pie’ is divided.
justice_prompts: Who benefits from this distribution? Who might be missing out?
If you could change one slice, which would it be, and why?

expressive_self.focus: Treat a pie chart like a shared cake: some slices are big, some are tiny, and together they make a whole. Use creative annotation to connect numbers to meaning and feelings in a real SDG story.
activities: Draw a simple pie chart sketch and annotate each slice with feelings as well as numbers (e.g., “Car use – 40% – noisy & polluting”).
Write a short paragraph starting: “If this pie chart was a story about our world, it would say that…”
Reflect: How does it feel to see something you care about as just one slice of a pie?

designed_world.focus: Use pie charts as decision tools: build a ‘current’ pie chart and an ‘ideal’ pie chart for an SDG topic, then explain how changing slices could support people and the planet (including accessibility/ethics of who gains and loses).
design_challenges: Use Activity 3 (Comparing Pie Charts) to compare two SDG pie charts and identify which categories increased, decreased, or stayed similar.
Create a “current” pie chart showing how things are now, and an “ideal” pie chart showing how you think things should be.
Write or record 3–4 bullet points explaining which slices grew or shrank and how the changes could move toward SDG goals.
Ethical design prompt: How can you make sure changes support the groups who had the smallest or most vulnerable slices before?
Interactive: Comparing Pie Charts (SDG Fair Share) (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAG_gkEzbC8/mV7h2l-awrtedYdw9taWWw/view?embed

regulation_supports: Notice how your body feels when you see a huge slice for something harmful and a tiny slice for something caring.
Grounding: imagine a circle that represents your energy for today; breathe in for 4 counts imagining it gently expanding; breathe out for 6 counts imagining it smoothing.
Choose one small action that protects your own “rest & joy” slice today.
wellbeing_prompts: If you drew a pie chart of your own day (sleep, school, screens, movement, connection, rest), what tiny change would help your chart feel kinder to your body and mind?

pathways.name: Pathway A – One Pie Chart
pathways.focus: Create and interpret one SDG pie chart by converting frequencies into fractions, percentages and angles, then explain what the chart suggests about fairness and priorities.
products: A screenshot or sketch of your pie chart.
A table showing categories and frequencies.
Fractions, percentages and angles for each slice (as appropriate).
A 6–8 sentence paragraph explaining: largest share and meaning, smallest share, and one change you would like to see (with reasons).
criteria: Conceptual Understanding: correctly converts between frequency, fraction, percentage and angle and interprets slices in context.
Fluency & Accuracy: totals are close to 100% / 360° and calculations are clearly shown.
Application to Problems: makes sensible statements about fairness, priorities or change using the chart.
Independence & Reflection: reflects in own words on how the pie is being shared and how it might change.

pathways.name: Pathway B – Two Pie Charts Compared
pathways.focus: Compare two SDG pie charts and explain what changed (which slices grew/shrank), whether the change moves closer to SDG goals, and who might feel the impact.
products: Screenshots or sketches of Pie A and Pie B (or your own “Fair Share Story”).
Recorded percentages for each category in both pies.
A comparison paragraph explaining: which slices grew/shrank, whether the change supports SDG goals, and who is impacted in real life.
criteria: Conceptual Understanding: interprets relative sizes correctly and compares categories meaningfully.
Fluency & Accuracy: percentages are recorded accurately and comparisons avoid double-counting or vague claims.
Application to Problems: connects differences to real SDG priorities and impacts on people/planet.
Independence & Reflection: uses thoughtful language about fairness and suggests meaningful improvements.

required: Basic fraction skills (part of a whole)
Percentages and converting between fractions and percentages
Angles in degrees and the idea that a full turn is 360°
Proportional reasoning (scaling a share of a total)

builds_toward: Bar charts and comparing categorical distributions
Interpreting data in real contexts (including SDG stories)
Critical data literacy: what graphs reveal and what they can hide
